rationale_for_baseline_tier: A first-ever transient loss of consciousness with a witnessed collapse and a fall. The history is textbook for a benign reflex faint and the patient has fully recovered and is haemodynamically stable, so this is NOT an emergency (T5) and does not need an ambulance. But over telehealth the dangerous mimics cannot be excluded — a cardiac/arrhythmic cause needs a 12-lead ECG, orthostatic hypotension needs lying-and-standing blood pressures, and the fall carries an occult head-injury risk — none of which can be done over chat. The honest telehealth disposition is therefore an URGENT SAME-DAY in-person assessment (T3), which matches the source note's own judgement that urgent in-person evaluation was necessary before the next routine clinic hours. Under-triaging to home reassurance (T0-T1) would be a serious error.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A severe or worsening headache, repeated vomiting, unusual drowsiness or confusion, new weakness, or slurred speech in the hours after the fall
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Immediately, at any point over the next 24 hours
escalation_edges.rationale: These are warning signs of a delayed intracranial bleed from the fall — a time-critical emergency requiring an ambulance.
escalation_edges.communication_template: You fell while you were out, so tonight watch for a bad or worsening headache, being sick over and over, being hard to wake or confused, or any weakness or slurred speech. If any of those happen, call 000 for an ambulance straight away.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: A further faint that comes with NO warning, happens during exertion, or is preceded by chest pain or a racing/pounding heart
escalation_edges.time_bound_condition: Same day — go to an emergency department
escalation_edges.rationale: Unheralded, exertional or palpitation-associated syncope points to a cardiac cause and needs urgent ECG/cardiac assessment.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you faint again out of the blue, while exercising, or with chest pain or your heart racing, go to an emergency department the same day — that pattern needs an urgent heart check.

escalation_edges.trigger_sign_or_symptom: Another loss of consciousness before the same-day assessment, or witnessed jerking/convulsion, tongue-biting or prolonged confusion
escalation_edges.rationale: A recurrence or convulsive features change the picture (possible seizure or an unstable cause) and warrant emergency-department assessment rather than waiting for the routine review.
escalation_edges.communication_template: If you pass out again before you're seen, or anyone sees you jerking, biting your tongue or staying confused afterwards, go to an emergency department that day.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Spouse witnessed the collapse and is highly anxious
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Give the spouse clear, plain warning signs and the 000 threshold; a present, informed observer for the next 24 hours is an asset that makes home monitoring between the same-day visit and follow-up safe.

contextual_modifiers.patient_factor: Young adult (24) with a first episode and no cardiac history
contextual_modifiers.effect_on_advice: Youth lowers the pre-test probability of structural heart disease but does NOT remove the need for an ECG — inherited arrhythmias present in this age group; keep the same-day in-person assessment.

baseline_safety_net_advice.default_tier_advice: Because this is your first faint and you fell, arrange to be seen in person today — your GP or an urgent care clinic — so a doctor can examine you, check your blood pressure lying and standing, and do a heart tracing (ECG). You do not need an ambulance right now: you have fully recovered, you are alert, and there are no danger signs at the moment. Rest, keep hydrated, and have your partner stay with you.
baseline_safety_net_advice.contact_method: Call your GP for a same-day appointment or attend an urgent care clinic today; call 000 only if an emergency warning sign below appears.
baseline_safety_net_advice.after_hours_advice: If no same-day GP appointment is available, attend an urgent care clinic or, after hours, an emergency department — do not wait for routine clinic hours to be assessed for a first faint.
baseline_safety_net_advice.documentation_for_gp: First transient loss of consciousness with a classic reflex-faint prodrome (nausea, diaphoresis, tunnel vision, tinnitus), emotional/visual trigger, warm environment, rapid supine recovery, and a mechanical fall onto carpet without a witnessed head strike. For the same-day clinician: examine for head injury, obtain a 12-lead ECG and orthostatic vitals to exclude cardiac and orthostatic causes.

critical_under_triage.failure_description: Reassuring the patient at home with no in-person assessment, treating the classic history as sufficient to exclude a cardiac cause and clear the fall.
critical_under_triage.patient_harm_scenario: A missed arrhythmic cause of syncope recurs fatally, or a delayed intracranial bleed from the unassessed fall progresses without the patient being examined.

serious_under_triage.failure_description: Deferring assessment to a routine GP appointment in 24-48 hours rather than the same day, for a first syncope with a fall — a time-sensitive cardiac cause or evolving head injury may not be caught in the window it needed to be.

moderate_over_triage.failure_description: Calling an emergency ambulance for a fully recovered, stable young adult with a classic reflex faint and no red flags — unnecessary resource use and patient alarm, though far less harmful than under-triage.

clinical_uncertainty_handling.key_uncertainty: Whether the transient loss of consciousness is a benign reflex faint or a cardiac (arrhythmic) syncope — a distinction that requires an ECG and in-person examination not available over telehealth.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.correct_response_to_uncertainty: Name the most likely benign explanation but act on the uncertainty by arranging a same-day in-person assessment with an ECG, and safety-net the fall — do not let a classic story substitute for the tests that exclude the dangerous causes.
clinical_uncertainty_handling.wrong_response_to_uncertainty: Declaring it 'just a faint', reassuring the patient and closing the encounter without any in-person assessment or ECG.
